attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with increasing confusion. She was admitted to the gastroenterology ward four days ago for assessment of recently diagnosed Child's B cirrhosis. She has a medical history of alcohol misuse, hepatitis C and chronic back pain. Her medications include propranolol, furosemide, spironolactone, and sustained-release oxycodone.

On examination, she appears drowsy. Her pulse rate is 102/min, blood pressure 120/88 mmHg, respiratory rate 12/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. She is disorientated to time and place with asterixis. Her abdomen is distended with shifting dullness. Digital rectal examination reveals dark blood. Her pupils are 6 mm bilaterally and reactive. She is producing 50 mL/hr of urine.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 98 g/L (115-165)
  • Platelets 85 x 10⁹/L (150-400)
  • International Normalised Ratio 1.8 (0.9-1.2)
  • Sodium 130 mmol/L (137-145)
  • Potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
  • Urea 23 mmol/L (2.7-8.0)
  • Creatinine 90 µmol/L (50-120)
  • Albumin 28 g/L (36-46)
  • Bilirubin 33 µmol/L (2-24)
  • Alanine transaminase 65 U/L (5-40)
  • Alkaline phosphatase 120 U/L (30-110)
  • Gamma-glutamyl transferase 180 U/L (5-50)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hepatic encephalopathy, likely precipitated by an upper gastrointestinal bleed as evidenced by tachycardia, dark blood on rectal examination, and elevated urea-to-creatinine ratio. Blood in the gastrointestinal tract acts as a protein load, which when metabolised leads to increased ammonia and worsening encephalopathy.

Lactulose 30 mL oral solution is the most appropriate first step as it rapidly reduces ammonia levels through its osmotic laxative effect. This addresses the acute hepatic encephalopathy while arrangements are made for further management of the likely upper GI bleed.

500 mL 0.9% sodium chloride bolus would be inappropriate as patients with cirrhosis and ascites are sensitive to sodium loading, which can worsen fluid retention. If the patient becomes haemodynamically unstable, blood products would be more appropriate.

100 mcg intravenous naloxone would be indicated for opioid toxicity, which typically presents with pinpoint pupils. This patient's 6 mm reactive pupils make opioid toxicity unlikely.

Oral diazepam 10 mg would be appropriate for alcohol withdrawal, which typically occurs within 24 hours of abstinence and presents with agitation rather than drowsiness. At day four of admission, alcohol withdrawal is unlikely.

Oral rifaximin 550 mg is used for prevention of recurrent hepatic encephalopathy in patients already on lactulose. While it may be beneficial long-term, it is not the most appropriate acute intervention.
learningPoints: Hepatic encephalopathy commonly occurs due to precipitants such as gastrointestinal bleeding, with blood acting as a protein load.
Lactulose is first-line treatment for acute hepatic encephalopathy.
Upper GI bleeding should be suspected in cirrhotic patients who develop confusion, especially with tachycardia and elevated urea:creatinine ratio.
references: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited. Available from: https://www.tg.org.au
Wijdicks EF. Hepatic Encephalopathy. The New England journal of medicine. 2016;375(17):1660–70

Butterworth R. Hepatic encephalopathy in alcoholic cirrhosis. Handbook of Clinical Neurology [Internet]. 2014 [cited 4 January 2022];125:589-602. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25307598/t
